coredata.dc:title: Emissive radiodense stealth plasmonic nanohybrid as X-ray contrast and photo-ablative agent of cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Jinal M.
Jain, Nishant K.
Chauhan, Deepak S.
Prasad, Rajendra
Kumawat, Mukesh K.
Dhanka, Mukesh
Shanavas, Asifkhan
Srivastava, Rohit
coredata.dc:description: Biodegradation and site-selective theranostic ability are critical concerns in cancer nanomedicine. Herein, we report a biodegradable plasmonic nanohybrid called H-PhotoFluor towards augmenting the dynamic contrast ability, focalized tumor imaging, and efficient ablation of cancer cells. Integrated hyaluronic acid (HA) targeting ligand, gold nanoshells, and fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induce the multimodality in engineered nanohybrid. The ridges of deposited gold nanoparticles help in the efficient binding of HA that augments the entrapment of GQDs. The active targeting here is achieved with the help of HA coating that selectively targets CD44 receptors. The pivotal element in focused imaging is attained by GQDs. These photothermally active radiodense engineered H-PhotoFluor (∼140 nm) have been tested for targeted tumor imaging and site-selective cancer cell destruction. Hemocompatibility, biocompatibility, biodegradability, photothermal activity and cellular internalization of H-PhotoFluor have been studied systematically. The observed outcomes of the aforementioned studies make H-PhotoFluor, a clinically relevant nanotheranostic agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanohybrid
Gold nanoparticles
Hyaluronic acid
Graphene quantum dots
Multimode imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of glycol chitosan functionalized PLGA nanoparticles (GC-PLGA NPs)
Ex-situ gold coating of GC-PLGA NPs
Synthesis of gold seeds and gold hydroxide
Functionalization of AuPLGA NS with HA and GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the synthesis and preclinical applications of H-PhotoFluor.

Micrographs of PLGA NPs, AuPLGA NPs, GQDs, and H-PhotoFluor. a) FEG-SEM image of GC-PLGA NPs; inset-zoomed image. b) FEG-TEM image of gold seeds; inset-zoomed image. c) TEM image of AuPLGA NS; inset-zoomed image. d) FEG-TEM image of GQDs with size around 4-7 nm; inset- zoomed image. e) FEG-TEM image of H-PhotoFluor. f, j) Zoomed image of H-PhotoFluor showing the layer of HA, GQDs, and gold nanoparticles. g) Diffraction pattern of AuPLGA NS. h) Diffraction pattern of GQDs. i) Diffraction pattern of H-PhotoFluor. k, l, m) Size distribution of PLGA NPs (k), AuPLGA NS (l), H-PhotoFluor (m).

Physiochemical characterization of H-PhotoFluor. a) The hydrodynamic diameter of H-PhotoFluor and controls. b) Change in zeta potential due to surface functionalization of PLGA NPs. c) Comparative absorbance of AuPLGA NS and H-PhotoFluor. d) Fluorescence of H-PhotoFluor. e) Comparative Raman spectra of AuPLGA NS and H-PhotoFluor. f) Raman spectra of H-PhotoFluor showing the presence of D and G band of GQDs. g) XPS analysis of H-PhotoFluor confirming the presence of GQDs on the surface, (i) C 1s, and (ii) O 1s XPS spectrum. h) XRD analysis of H-PhotoFluor.

Biocompatibility of H-PhotoFluor. a) Degradation of H-PhotoFluor after incubating at physiological conditions (left) and laser irradiation (right); corresponding EDX analysis (bottom). b) Microscopic images of MTT crystals formed in L929 cells incubated with H-PhotoFluor; scale bar 100 μm c) Percentage biocompatibility of H-PhotoFluor determined with the help of MTT assay. d) ESEM images and EDAX analysis of RBCs incubated with PC (positive control), NC (negative control), and H-PhotoFluor. e) Percentage hemolytic potential of H-PhotoFluor in respect to the positive and negative control.

Intracellular localization of H-PhotoFluor in MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells. a) Inverted microscopic images of MDA-MB-231 cells without any treatment, AuPLGA NS treated, GQDs treated, and H-PhotoFluor treated; red triangle showing the rimming of H-PhotoFluor on the cell surface; magnification power 10X (scale bar 250 μm) and 40X (scale bar 20 μm). b) Bright field, dark field, and merged confocal images of MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with H-PhotoFluor. c) The optical section with x, y axes, and corresponding projections of x, z, and y, z axes of MCF-7 cells. d, e, f) 2.5D and 3D intracellular imaging showing an obvious localization signals from cytoplasm stained with H-PhotoFluor. g) FACS showing the uptake of H-PhotoFluor. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Photothermal characteristics of H-PhotoFluor. a) Phototransduction potential of H-PhotoFluor. b) Quantitative analysis of H-PhotoFluor driven photothermal therapy in MDA-MB 231 and MCF-7 cells. c) Bright and dark field images of H-PhotoFluor driven photothermal therapy in MCF-7 cells. d) Thermal imaging of H-PhotoFluor, AuPLGA NS, GQDs, and PLGA NPs.
a) FDXM images of control and H-PhotoFluor embedded agarose phantom. b) Quantitative assessment of X-ray attenuation potential of H-PhotoFluor.

head.ce:title: Emissive radiodense stealth plasmonic nanohybrid as X-ray contrast and photo-ablative agent of cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Powai, Mumbai, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Powai, Mumbai, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST) Mohali, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST) Mohali

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST) Mohali, Punjab, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodegradation and site-selective theranostic ability are critical concerns in cancer nanomedicine. Herein, we report a biodegradable plasmonic nanohybrid called H-PhotoFluor towards augmenting the dynamic contrast ability, focalized tumor imaging, and efficient ablation of cancer cells. Integrated hyaluronic acid (HA) targeting ligand, gold nanoshells, and fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induce the multimodality in engineered nanohybrid. The ridges of deposited gold nanoparticles help in the efficient binding of HA that augments the entrapment of GQDs. The active targeting here is achieved with the help of HA coating that selectively targets CD44 receptors. The pivotal element in focused imaging is attained by GQDs. These photothermally active radiodense engineered H-PhotoFluor (∼140 nm) have been tested for targeted tumor imaging and site-selective cancer cell destruction. Hemocompatibility, biocompatibility, biodegradability, photothermal activity and cellular internalization of H-PhotoFluor have been studied systematically. The observed outcomes of the aforementioned studies make H-PhotoFluor, a clinically relevant nanotheranostic agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent developments in the treatment of cancer involve the use of alternative remedies like the use of phytochemicals, synthetic chemotherapeutics, immunogens etc [1,2]. A plethora of nanomaterials-driven therapies have also evolved which includes magnetic hyperthermia, use of polymeric nanomaterials, cargo loaded lipid nanocarriers and much more [3]. However, the issues of biocompatibility, focalized imaging, degradation, and cost of synthesis remain the limiting factors in their commercialization [4]. In an effort to overcome these off-putting aspects, we have explored the dynamics of photothermal therapy (PTT). It is recognized as a non-invasive to a minimally invasive technique for controlled ablation of cancer cells upon the irradiation by near-infrared (NIR) light [5,6]. It exploits the property of electromagnetic waves wherein the plasmonic nanomaterials are used to generate localized heat which brings about the cancer cell death. In clinical terms, the heat that is produced is known as hyperthermia (≥43 °C) [5,6].

For being an ideal photothermal agent, the particles must demonstrate excellent biocompatibility, uniformity, prominent absorption spectra in the NIR region, targeting potential and non-toxicity. 2-D nanomaterials that are being currently investigated include antimonene quantum dots, silicon NP, phosphorus, mxene, boron nanosheets etc [7–13]. However, owing to their uneven distribution and low therapeutic potential their applications are restricted. Inorganic materials possess unique properties which can be utilized to design nanohybrids for imaging and drug delivery applications. These features make them ideal for clinical use in the diagnosis, detection and treatment of various diseases. Inorganic nanomaterials including silica, silver, gold etc. are investigated in preclinical as well as clinical trial phases. Gold nanoparticles termed auroshells synthesized by Prof. Naomi Halas group are also under clinical trial. Aurimune is a TNF-based gold NP and has finished the clinical trial phase and is overlaying the road for the development of nanoparticle based targeted therapies [14]. Hence, gold nanoshells with distinctive plasmonic structures having tunable absorbance capabilities from the visible to the NIR region are extensively used [15]. Further, different classes of biomaterials such as lipid-liposomes, semiconductors-silicon oxides, zein, polymers- polycaprolactone (PCL), poly (lactic-co-glycolic) acid (PLGA), poly N-vinyl caprolactam (PNVCL) etc. have been used in conjunction with gold nanoparticles towards the development of multifunctional plasmonic nanotheranostics [16–20]. Amidst different polymers used, PLGA is found to be ideal as it is an FDA approved polymer with excellent biocompatibility and biodegradation capabilities [21]. Thus, further advancement of PLGA plasmonic nanoshells is well justified for the development of efficient and safe photothermal agents.

The effect of the nanomaterial-generated hyperthermia on cancer cells is pleiotropic and complex [6]. Thus, to ensure safety as well as the efficiency of the photothermal ablation, real-time confined imaging is critically required for improving the PTT regimens [22]. The multimodal real-time focalized imaging could help in delineating and irradiating the tumor in a more localized fashion [23]. The widely used hyperthermic agents for image-guided study include iron oxide nanoparticles functionalized with graphene oxide (GO-IONP), metal catalyst conjugated carbon nanotubes (Metal-SWCNT) etc. Such nanomaterials possess complex surface chemistry, poor solubility, and limited biodegradation in physiological conditions [24–27]. The shortcomings of such agents is met by the use of NIR fluorescent materials like indocyanine green (ICG), IR780, IR820 etc. Nevertheless, even they are susceptible to photobleaching and render cellular toxicity in a dose-dependent manner [16]. Besides, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials that serve as a potential agent for bioimaging [28,29]. Not only are they biocompatible and stable but are also not vulnerable to photobleaching. They fluoresce for a longer time, involve low-cost synthesis and most importantly, do not leach to the nearby tissues [29]. The photoluminescence property of GQDs is widely explored in the field of bioimaging and sensing due to their narrow absorbance and wide emission spectral characteristics [23,30].

The targeting of photothermal nanotherapeutics (PTNs) is an essential criterion for an effective therapy [18]. The leaky vasculature at the tumor site helps in the passive accumulation of nanomaterials due to enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR). However, in most cases, the percentage enrichment is very less for experiencing the therapeutic effect at a safe dosage amount. Thus, active targeting is implemented to target the overexpressed receptors on the surface of cancer cells. For example, folate, CD44, epidermal growth factor (EGF), and transferrin receptors (TfR) are among the most promising targets which help in providing selectivity of malignant cells over the healthy ones [30,31]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) has been of much interest to the researchers due to its inherent ability to bind to CD44 receptors overexpressed on tumor cells [32]. Further, HA is highly hydrophilic, non-immunogenic, and biodegradable in nature [33].

Thus, the focus of our study is to fabricate a nanohybrid system which could help in real-time imaging as well as ablate the malignant cells in a highly controlled manner. The controlled growth of gold shell over soft polymeric core via a slow reduction process is demonstrated and further functionalized with biocompatible HA as a targeting ligand and emissive GQDs as imaging probes. The synthesized nanoshells (HA-GQD_AuPLGA NS called as H-PhotoFluor) were subjected to various morphological, physicochemical, and spectroscopic studies to characterize their surface charge, uniformity, elemental composition, concentration, absorbance maxima, crystallinity etc. Thorough biocompatibility, hemolysis as well as cellular internalization studies were also performed. Additionally, the ability to generate photothermal response was meticulously investigated.

Poly(lactic-co-glycolic) acid (PLGA, 50:50) was gifted from Purac Biochemical, Netherlands. Tocopherol polyethylene glycol 1000 succinate (Vitamin E TPGS) and glycol chitosan (GC) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, India. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), formaldehyde, and glutaraldehyde were procured from Merck, India. Tetrakis (hydroxymethyl) phosphonium chloride (THPC) was purchased from Acros Organics, Belgium. Hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HACl) was obtained from Fischer Scientific, USA. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye, trypsin-EDTA, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and antibiotic-antimycotic (streptomycin-penicillin) mixture were bought from HiMedia Laboratories, India. Hyaluronic acid was a kind gift by Shiseido Co. Ltd., Japan. High purity Milli-Q water system (Millipore, USA) supplied the water for carrying out experiments. All glassware were cleaned with aqua regia (freshly prepared) and thoroughly rinsed with Milli-Q water before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of glycol chitosan functionalized PLGA nanoparticles (GC-PLGA NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: GC-PLGA NPs synthesis was carried out by the solvent evaporation method [17,34]. In brief, the organic phase comprised of 25 mg PLGA dissolved in 10 mL acetonitrile, whereas the aqueous phase consisted of 20 mg glycol chitosan dissolved in 0.02 % Vitamin E TPGS. Both the solutions were allowed to stir (REMI 2MLH) overnight. The organic phase was added into the aqueous phase using a syringe pump (New Era Pump Systems Corp) at a flow rate of 100 μL/min. The mixture was left for stirring at 1200 rpm for 2 h at room temperature and pressure. After stirring, sonication (130 Sonics Vibra Cell) at 40 % amplitude was performed for 5 min. The sonicated mixture was collected in a beaker, and to evaporate the acetonitrile the emulsion was kept for overnight stirring. Bath sonication was performed for 1 min to redisperse the NPs before centrifugation. GC-PLGA NPs formed were pelleted down using a centrifuge machine (Hitachi CF15RXII) at 15,000 rpm for 60 min at 4 °C. Pellet was resuspended in Milli-Q water and washed thrice. Eventually, the collected nanoparticles were dispersed in 30 mL Milli-Q water and stored at 4 °C. The two-step sonication process involving the probe as well as bath sonication steps results in the production of uniform NPs as over the traditional one-step procedure [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex-situ gold coating of GC-PLGA NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The ex-situ gold coating of GC-PLGA NPs was performed using the following steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gold seeds and gold hydroxide
ce:section.ce:para: The gold seeds were prepared at ambient conditions, as described previously [6]. In brief, 9 mL of Milli-Q water, 100 μL of 1 M NaOH, and 200 μL of 1.2 mM THPC solutions were mixed and stirred at high speed for 5 min. Next, 400 μL of 100 mM HAuCl4 solution was added and stirred vigorously for another 10 min. The color of the solution changed to dark brown, which indicates the formation of gold seeds of size 2−5 nm. Gold hydroxide solution was prepared by solubilizing 10.8 mg of K2CO3 in 40 mL Milli-Q water with the subsequent addition of 3200 μL of 5 mM HAuCl4. It was aged for 24 h in the dark at room temperature.

The gold shell was formed on GC-PLGA NPs, as described previously [6]. Briefly, gold seeds synthesized were incubated with the GC-PLGA NPs and kept for overnight incubation in the dark at room temperature. The next day, the mixture was pelleted down at 4000 rpm for 60 min at 4 °C. It was washed thrice, and the pellet was resuspended in 2 mL Milli-Q water. In a beaker, 15 mL of gold hydroxide, 5 mL of 1.87 mM HACl, and 1.5 mL of gold seeds attached GC-PLGA NPs were added under moderate stirring. The color changed to dark blue within 1−2 min. 25 mL gold hydroxide was added intermittently in fractions (each of 5 mL) along with 3 mL of HACl with incessant stirring as per the desired absorbance spectra. After 1 h, the absorbance was recorded, and gold deposited GC-PLGA NPs called AuPLGA NS were pelleted down at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. at 4 °C. It was resuspended in 10 mL of Milli-Q water and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of AuPLGA NS with HA and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared via a green synthesis method using Mangifera indica leaves, as described previously [28]. In a beaker filled with 10 mL Milli-Q water, 10 mg HA and 10 mg GQDs were added to prepare a 1 mg/mL solution. It was strongly stirred for 30 min, thereafter, 3 mL of AuPLGA NS were added. After overnight stirring, HA and GQDs functionalized AuPLGA NS called H-PhotoFluor was centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C. The pellet was resuspended in 1 mL Milli-Q water and stored at 4 °C.

The biocompatibility of the sample was determined using MTT assay and hemolysis test. In a 96-well plate, 200 μL of DMEM containing 10 % FBS and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic (streptomycin-penicillin) mixture (cDMEM) containing 8000 cells (L929) were seeded per well. After overnight incubation, the spent media from each well was discarded. Different concentrations of H-PhotoFluor dispersed in cDMEM were added to the wells. After 24 h of incubation, the content of each well was discarded. 100 μL of 0.5 % MTT solution was added and left for 4 h of incubation. The content from the wells was discarded, and 200 μL of DMSO was added to solubilize the formazan crystals. The absorbance was determined at 570 nm and 690 nm using a Tecan Infinite 200 PRO plate reader. Percentage cell viability was determined in reference to the controls.

Wistar rat’s blood was obtained from Bombay Veterinary College, India after getting ethical committee clearance, and hemolysis studies were performed according to ISO/TR 7406. Briefly, 200 μL of H-PhotoFluor was pelleted down and resuspended in 200 μL of PBS. Then, equal amounts of red blood cells (RBCs) (separated from the blood by repeated centrifugation at 1000 rpm at room temperature) were added to it, and the mixture was kept in a shaker incubator for 2 h at 37 °C. The mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min and then 100 μL of the supernatant was taken out for colorimetric readings at 540 nm [36,37]. The percentage hemolysis was calculated in reference to the controls as per the following equation:
%
H e m o l y s i s =
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
t e s t
s a m p l e s
( 540
n m )
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
( 540
n m )
× 100

RBCs treated with Triton X and PBS were used as a positive and negative control, respectively. For environmental scanning electron microscope (E-SEM) imaging, 1 mL of 2 % glutaraldehyde solution was added to the RBCs and nanoparticle mixture. After gentle mixing, it was incubated overnight at 4 °C. The mixture was pelleted down at 4000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was discarded, and the pellet was gently suspended in 500 μL Milli-Q water. 10 μL of this diluted mixture was drop cast on the ESEM stubs, and images were captured at both normal and backscattering mode.

The qualitative cellular uptake studies were performed using Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM, Olympus FluoView). MDA-MB 231 cells (breast cancer cell line) were used for the study. In a 12 well plate, round coverslips were put into each well. They were washed thrice with PBS and kept under UV light for 30 min. 1 mL of 20,000–22,000 cells suspended in cDMEM were seeded per well. After overnight incubation, the content of each well was emptied without disturbing the coverslips. 200 μL of H-PhotoFluor dispersed in 1 mL of cDMEM was added in the respective wells and incubated for 24 h in a CO2 incubator. The content of each well was discarded without disturbing the cells and washed thrice with PBS, thereafter 1 mL of 3.7 % formaldehyde was added to fix cells. After incubating in a CO2 incubator for 15 min, formaldehyde from each well was washed with PBS in a repetitive manner. The washed coverslips were mounted over the glass slide using 10 % glycerol and nail paint. CLSM imaging was performed using a 561 nm laser (red filter) [29].

The quantitative cellular uptake studies were performed using flow cytometry (BD FACS Aria III). MDA-MB 231 cells were cultured in T-25 flasks with a cell density of ∼1 × 106 cells. 3 mL of media having nanoparticles concentration of 200 μg/mL was added into each flask. After 24 h, the utilized media was discarded, and cells were trypsinized and washed thrice. The final pellet was resuspended in 500 μL PBS for flow cytometry. The excitation wavelength used was 637 nm, and the emission wavelength was 670 nm with a band pass filter of 15 nm cutoff range. 10,000 cells from samples were measured for the fluorescence intensities [29]. GQDs were used as a positive control, and untreated MDA-MB 231 cells were used as a negative control. The data was processed with the help of FlowJo software.

200 μL of the sample was added in triplicates in a 96 well plate maintained at 37 °C. 750 nm NIR laser (650 mW, Shanghai Dream Lasers) was irradiated on the particles up to 10 min. The temperature was monitored every minute with the help of a digital thermometer (Oakton Mini-InfraPro, Cole-Parmer) as well as a thermal imaging camera (FLIR E5). Milli-Q water was used as a reference. The temperature escalation was plotted against the laser irradiation time.

Photothermal therapy was performed on MCF-7 and MDA-MB 231 cells. 200 μL of cDMEM containing 8000 cells was seeded per wells in 96 well plate. After overnight incubation, the spent media from each well was discarded and different concentrations of H-PhotoFluor dispersed in cDMEM were added in triplicates to the wells. After 6 h of incubation, 750 nm NIR laser light was irradiated on respective wells for 5 min and the plate was again incubated for 24 h. The wells were emptied, and 100 μL of 0.5 % MTT solution was added and left for 4 h of incubation. The content from the wells was discarded, and 200 μL of DMSO was added to solubilize the formazan crystals. Afterwards, absorbance was determined at 570 nm and 690 nm using a Tecan Infinite 200 PRO plate reader. Percentage viability was determined in reference to the controls. The qualitative analysis was also performed similarly with the help of propidium iodide in MCF-7 cells. Briefly, 0.5 mM of propidium iodide was added after 24 h of photothermal therapy, and the plate was kept back in the incubator for 15 min. After that, wells were rinsed with PBS, and the images were captured using the fluorescent microscope (Nikon Ti Eclipse) at the PI channel.

The hydrodynamic diameter and polydispersity index (PDI) of the NPs were evaluated with the help of dynamic light scattering (DLS, Brookhaven Instruments Corporation). The surface charge was determined using Zeta Potential Analyzer (Malvern Panalytical). Morphological characterization was performed with the help of a field emission gun-scanning electron microscope (FEG-SEM, JSM 7600 F JEOL) and field emission gun-transmission electron microscope (FEG-TEM, JEOL 2100 F). For SEM and TEM sample preparation, samples were loaded on copper grids/copper stubs by drop cast method. They were kept in a vacuumed desiccator until dry and also exposed to IR light for 10 min before loading on grids. The absorbance of the samples was recorded using a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer). Phospholuminescence measurement was carried out using a spectrofluorometer (Perkin-Elmer). The GQDs and H-PhotoFluor were excited at 450 nm, and emission was recorded from 475 nm to 750 nm. To determine the concentration of gold in H-PhotoFluor, inductively coupled plasmon-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, SPECTRO Analytical Instrument) was performed. Briefly, 50 μL of the sample was added to 1 mL of aqua regia and left overnight at room temperature. The mixture was diluted with 14 mL of Milli-Q water before analysis. For Raman spectroscopy, 10 mg of lyophilized sample was used for analysis. A wide and narrow spectrum shift was recorded from 100 to 3000 and 1100 to 2000 wavenumbers, respectively on the FT-Raman spectrometer (Bruker RFS 27-Multi RAM, Germany). Four-dimensional X-ray microscopy (FDXM, Zeiss-Xradia 520) was performed to check the contrast ability of H-PhotoFluor, and images obtained were further processed using ImageJ and Fiji.

Fig. 2 shows the particle size distribution and microscopic analysis. GC-PLGA NPs were found to be monodisperse with a size range of 90−130 nm ( Fig. 2 a-k). Prepared gold seeds with better distribution were shown in Fig. 2 b. The morphological characterization shows that AuPLGA NS were spherical in shape with a diameter of around 140 nm ( Fig. 2 c, l). The concentration of gold incorporated in AuPLGA NS was estimated to be 125−170 μg/mL (Table S1). The average micrographic sizes of AuPLGA NS were determined to be ∼ 140 nm, whereas that of GQDs was found to be 4−7 nm as shown in Fig. 2 d. However, a slight increase in the particle size of H-PhotoFluor (∼ 150 nm) was noticed due to HA coating over the AuPLGA NS ( Fig. 2 e, f). Thus, we can say that HA forms a layer of ∼ 10 nm ( Fig. 2 j). In the surface area electron diffraction pattern of H-PhotoFluor, the lattice fringes of gold and GQDs were identified with the disturbing lattice pattern of gold’s 12 or 24 further confirms the embedding of GQDs in HA layer deposited over AuPLGA NS (Fig.2 g, h, i). The percentage size distribution of formulations was also analysed, wherein less heterogeneous populations were observed owing to low PDI (Table S2, Fig. 2 k, l, m and Fig. S1).

Physicochemical parameters of the designed nanohybrid are shown in Fig. 3 . The hydrodynamic diameter of the nanoparticles was also analyzed using dynamic light scattering (DLS), which was found to be consistent with the results achieved by TEM and SEM ( Fig. 3 a). The assembly of each component changed the surface charge of NPs (Fig. 3 b). The positively charged GC-PLGA NPs changed to negative with a zeta potential (ZP) of -17.2 ± 5.27 mV due to THPC reduced gold seeds [6]. The ZP value of HA_AuPLGA NS (-36.4 ± 6.88 mV) and H-PhotoFluor (-54.6 ± 6.97 mV) was also negative due to the carboxyl group of d-glucuronic acid present in HA [41]. The increase in negative zeta potential after functionalization by different moieties proves the stability of H-PhotoFluor. Further, the gold nanoparticles on GC-PLGA NPs were deposited using the ex situ method, and the absorbance peak was narrowly tuned at 750 nm as shown in Fig. 3 c. The narrow absorbance in the NIR region is critical for an efficient rise in the temperature during photothermal therapy which in turn depends on the core to shell ratio of nanoshells. The change in the functionalization of particles is also reflected in the change in crystallinity due to the incorporation of GQDs. X-ray diffraction studies show the change in the crystallinity of nanoparticles from being highly amorphous to crystalline (as discussed below). It was further confirmed by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of AuPLGA NS, GQDs, and H-PhotoFluor ( Fig. 2 g, h, i).

In the UV–vis absorbance spectra, AuPLGA NS were found to show absorbance in the NIR region from 700−1000 nm with absorbance maxima at 750 nm ( Fig. 3 c). After coating with HA and GQDs, the absorbance peak blue-shifted to 700 nm. The narrowness of the peak can be attributed to the HA-GQDs coating which leads to a change in the core-shell ratio. Fluorescence studies were also carried out at excitation wavelength 450 nm and emission wavelength 475 nm. GQDs, as well as H-PhotoFluor, were found to fluoresce at 675 nm. However, no peak was observed for AuPLGA NS ( Fig. 3 d). The similarity in the fluorescence peak of H-PhotoFluor and GQDs implies the successful coating of GQDs over the AuPLGA nanoshell. It was further verified by the Raman spectrum, wherein the crystallinity of H-PhotoFluor was found similar to GQDs ( Fig. 3 e). The presence of functional groups creates disorders in the graphitic carbon skeleton where both kinds of hybridization, i.e., sp2 and sp3 are prevalent. For H-PhotoFluor, the disorder peak (D band) was observed at 1342 cm−1, and the graphitic peak (G band) was observed at 1587 cm−1(Fig. 3 f). The ID/IG ratio (i.e., 0.84) shows the presence of hybridized carbons of graphene material in the H-PhotoFluor [42]. While in AuPLGA NS, the D and G bands were not found. Further, XPS analysis confirmed the presence of carbon-oxygen bonds specific to graphene materials ( Fig. 3 g.i, g.ii), wherein CO and CO bonds characteristic to GQDs were clearly noticed [28]. Also, the peak at 24°θ obtained in XRD, confirmed the oxygen-bearing moieties and successful embedding of GQDs to HA-coated AuPLGA NS ( Fig. 3 h).

In order to leverage the commercial advantages of these nanohybrids, a thorough risk analysis needs to be done. A lot of studies are involved from in vitro to in vivo followed by preclinical and clinical studies until its commercial usage. Understanding the toxicology and biocompatibility is as important as comprehending the specificity and selectivity of the nanohybrids [43,44]. Thus, hemocompatibility, cell cytotoxicity, and biodegradation studies of engineered H-PhotoFluor were systematically done as shown in Fig. 4 . Notably, a comprehensive degradation study was carried out for H-PhotoFluor in physiological and laser irradiation conditions (therapeutic requirements in this work). The results showed the disintegration as well as clear degradation of the nanohybrid ( Fig. 4 a). On the focalized irradiation of NIR light, gold nanoparticles detached from the polymeric core while keeping the nanohybrid in physiological conditions (37 °C, sterile PBS) for a month lead to the clear degradation of the PLGA core. These detached gold nanoparticles have the potential for the renal clearance while the degradation product of PLGA core (i.e., lactic acid and glycolic acid) get absorbed in the metabolic cycles. The degradation study was further supported by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, wherein the distribution of gold and other elements were mapped. The gold was found to be present as a ring, while carbon was seen to be dispersed, which further verify the degradation of nanohybrid from the inside ( Fig. 4 a). Further, these detached small gold nanoparticles preferably distribute homogenously in the tumor microenvironment that could help in higher radiodensity and contrast ability with intricate 3D details of tumor microenvironment at very low X-ray dosage.

The biocompatibility studies are very important in the case of injected biomaterials for determining the toxicity profile. HA and GQDs concentration of H-PhotoFluor were optimized to find the best biocompatibility ( Fig. 4 c, lower panel). The biocompatibility studies were performed on L929 cells and RBCs of a rat. The percentage cell viability of L929 cells was determined using an MTT assay. HA at 1 mg/mL, as well as 2 mg/mL, did not show any cell death. While GQDs showed the dose-dependent toxicity which is common in the case of the graphene, e.g., 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL of GQDs, the percentage cell viability decreased to ∼ 95 %, ∼81 %, and ∼76 % respectively, the concentrations of GQDs and HA for the fabrication of H-PhotoFluor was used below 1 mg/mL. The 50 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL of H-PhotoFluor almost showed zero cytotoxicity. Also, more than 95 % of the cells were viable even after 24 h of incubation with 150 μg/mL of H-PhotoFluor. At 200 μg/mL, cell morphology seems slightly disturbed, but still, the viability was noticed to be above 85 %. The cell viability was cross-checked with the assurance of the formation of MTT crystals, as reducing agents used during the gold shell formation many times lead to the change in color without crystal formation if nanoshells aren’t washed off properly ( Fig. 4 b).

The hemolysis assay is critical for the intravenously injected nanomaterials. The percentage hemolysis at 100 μg/mL and 150 μg/mL of H-PhotoFluor were found to be less than 5% in comparison to the positive control ( Fig. 4 e). E-SEM images were captured, and EDX spectroscopy was also carried out. The morphology of RBCs was found to be intact even in the presence of nanoparticles ( Fig. 4 d). E-SEM images revealed very little affinity of H-PhotoFluor towards erythrocytes as only a few H-PhotoFluor found adhered to the membrane of RBCs. The low affinity could be attributed to the high negative zeta potential, i.e., -54.6 mV ( Fig. 3 b). The presence of H-PhotoFluor to the RBCs membrane was further verified with the help of EDAX, wherein the distinguished gold peak was observed. As per ISO/TR 7406, materials showing less than 5 % hemolysis are regarded as safe, and H-PhotoFluor falls well within the acceptable range.

Cellular internalization as well as surface adherence of H-PhotoFluor was studied using confocal laser scanning microscopy and light microscopy, respectively. It was observed that H-PhotoFluor did not show any significant aggregation similar to AuPLGA NS on the addition to the cells, which could be clearly attributed to its high negative charge. However, the black rimming of the cells boundary was observed that could be evidence of robust surface adherence towards efficient uptake of H-PhotoFluor in comparison to AuPLGA NS ( Fig. 5
a). In CLSM driven uptake study, untreated control (i.e., alone MDA-MB-231 cells) showed no fluorescence. While distinguished red fluorescence was seen in cells treated with H-PhotoFluor upon excitation with 561 nm laser ( Fig. 5 b, c). The intracellular localization was also studied in Z stacked images in the form of orthogonal, 2.5D, and 3D projections. The red fluorescing H-PhotoFluor can be seen in the x, y, and z axes in the cytoplasm of the cells. The focalized imaging potential of H-PhotoFluor can be experienced with the localized fluorescence spot in comparison to the diffused one that appears in the case of near infer-red fluorescence (NIRF) dyes ( Fig. 5 d, e, f).

Further, the quantitative uptake study was done by flow cytometry ( Fig. 5 g). The comparative histogram reveals the specific uptake of H-PhotoFluor in contrast to AuPLGA NS. Cells with GQDs showed only ∼ 15.3 % uptake, while cells incubated with H-PhotoFluor showed ∼ 46.72 % cell uptake. The higher percentage of H-PhotoFluor uptake could be evidently attributed to the CD44 receptor targeting ability of HA that are overexpressed on MDA-MB-231 cells ( Fig. 1 ). As seen in the studies with GQDs, the intensity of the signals do not substantially change with the change in temperature.31 Accordingly, photothermal therapy was designed.

Photothermal transduction and in vitro validations of H-PhotoFluor were investigated and its potential impact for phototherapy is shown in Fig. 6 . The hyperthermic temperature is responsible for irreversible cytotoxic damage to the cells [45]. Photothermal transduction study was performed to study the NIR light-driven heating profile of H-PhotoFluor. In 10 min of photothermal transduction study, it was observed that H-PhotoFluor underwent a constant increase in temperature from 37 °C to 49.5 °C within 4 min. of laser irradiation in reference to control, i.e., Milli-Q water, and subsequently, the temperature decreased gradually ( Fig. 6 a). This signifies that the rise in temperature was corresponding to the time of laser irradiation up to 4 min. Afterwards, saturation in the temperature curve occurs possibly due to the disintegration of nanoshells ( Fig. 4 a). Further, the phototransduction potential of H-PhotoFluor was cross-verified with the help of a thermal imaging camera ( Fig. 6 d). The temperature increment in the case of controls, e.g., PLGA NPs and GQDs, was near to the room temperature, and readings were matched with the probe thermometer. However, the H-PhotoFluor was found to show relatively less temperature raise than determined by the probe thermometer. It could be attributed to the disturbance by fluorescent GQDs, although further studies are required in this direction.

The photothermal therapy was carried out on breast cancer cell lines, e.g., MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells. The controls were untreated MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells, cells treated with laser only, and cells treated with 100 μg/mL of H-PhotoFluor only. In the wells, wherein cells were treated with 100 μg/mL of H-PhotoFluor for 6 h and then 5 min. of laser irradiation, 89.5 % of cell death was seen in MDA-MB-231 and 85.6 % in MCF-7 cells ( Fig. 6 b, c). Further, the photothermal killing potential of H-PhotoFluor was confirmed with the help of PI dye. It was found that the cells treated with both laser and H-PhotoFluor showed the intense killing of cells (marked by red fluorescence). While the cells treated with only laser or only H-PhotoFluor, the cell death was negligible or equivalent to control, which establishes the safe nature of NIR laser and H-PhotoFluor. Hence, H-PhotoFluor proves to be an efficient photothermal agent with localized killing competency and focalized imaging potential.

Gold nanoparticles also act as strong contrast agents due to their high X-ray attenuation power [46]. FDXM was performed to assess the CT imaging potential of H-PhotoFluor. It was found that H-PhotoFluor embedded phantom showed better contrast in comparison to control ( Fig. 7
a, b). The further processing of the data with ImageJ helped in seeing the inside of the tumor phantom, which could help the clinician in determining the optimum concentration of H-PhotoFluor and intensity of laser towards maximizing the therapeutic versus side-effects ratio (Supplementary movie). The specificity of H-PhotoFluor for selective and sensitive targeting of the cancer cells with the help of HA and enhanced contrast due to gold nanoparticles makes H-PhotoFluor as a targeted, localized contrast agent for CT imaging.

H-PhotoFluor is a biocompatible, biodegradable, and an efficient photothermal agent with distinguished imaging potential of both NIRF and CT imaging modalities. The focalized imaging is critically required for the precisely localized therapy, and H-PhotoFluor holds great potential towards it. H-PhotoFluor opens new horizons of multimodal focalized imaging directed localized photothermal therapy

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Department of Biotechnology Government of India under the Nanobiotechnology program (BT/PR21972/NNT/28/1280/2017) and Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) Government of India under Core Research Grant scheme (EMR/2016/003851). The authors are grateful to DST-INSPIRE, Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, New Delhi for providing the fellowship. The authors would also like to thank the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF) and Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Sciences (CRNTS), IIT Bombay for providing access to all the research facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Data of gold concentration in nanohybrid, PDI values, and size of gold seeds.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2021.102181.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
